You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

{"code": "import subprocess, base64, json

# Repos that use @ai-whisperers packages
repos = [
    "elviajero-comercio", "fun4me", "golden-visa-advisory", "superspuma",
    "dayah-litworks", "nudo", "granja-cabral", "3md-website",
    "depiflash", "de-abasto-a-casa", "polki-squad", "bufete-mendez",
    "stoicfinch", "nico-portfolio", "nexa-propiedades",
    "magnolia-peluqueria", "luis-de-leon-concept", "mantra-spa",
    "cocodrilo-fitness", "bichos-gym", "clinica-duerksen",
    "template-nextjs-client",
]

results = []
for repo in repos:
    r = subprocess.run(["gh", "api", f"repos/Ai-Whisperers/{repo}/contents/package.json"],
                       capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=15)
    if r.returncode != 0:
        results.append(f"{repo:30s} SKIP (no package.json)")
        continue
    
    data = json.loads(r.stdout)
    pkg = json.loads(base64.b64decode(data["content"]).decode())
    sha = data["sha"]
    
    deps = pkg.get("dependencies", {})
    dev_deps = pkg.get("devDependencies", {})
    changed = False
    
    # Replace file: paths with proper versions
    for dep_type, dep_dict in [("dependencies", deps), ("devDependencies", dev_deps)]:
        for k, v in list(dep_dict.items()):
            if k.startswith("@ai-whisperers/") and v.startswith("file:"):
                dep_dict[k] = "^0.1.0"
                changed = True
    
    # Add .npmrc if missing
    # Also add test-vitest if not present
    if "@ai-whisperers/test-vitest" not in deps and "@ai-whisperers/test-vitest" not in dev_deps:
        dev_deps["@ai-whisperers/test-vitest"] = "^0.1.0"
        # ensure vitest is there too
        if "vitest" not in deps and "vitest" not in dev_deps:
            dev_deps["vitest"] = "^3.0.0"
        changed = True
    
    if not changed:
        results.append(f"{repo:30s} OK (no file: deps)")
        continue
    
    new_content = json.dumps(pkg, indent=2)
    new_b64 = base64.b64encode(new_content.encode()).decode()
    
    r2 = subprocess.run([
        "gh", "api", f"repos/Ai-Whisperers/{repo}/contents/package.json",
        "--method", "PUT",
        "-f", "message=refactor: use @aiw packages from npm registry instead of file: paths",
        "-f", f"content={new_b64}",
        "-f", f"sha={sha}"
    ], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=20)
    
    ok = "OK" if r2.returncode == 0 else "FAIL"
    results.append(f"{repo:30s} {ok}")

for r in results:
    print(r)
"}

Here's the full audit of what's NOT extracted yet and what work remains:

---

**Extraction candidates for `@aiw/*` packages (96 components not yet packaged):**

| Package | Components | ROI |
|---|---|---|
| **@aiw/payments** | bancard, pagopar, stripe, paypal + factory | HIGH — every client needs this, complex payment libs |
| **@aiw/product** | add-to-cart, reviews, faq, tabs, gallery, magnifier, recently-viewed, frequently-bought, price-range (19 total) | HIGH — shared by every commerce site |
| **@aiw/checkout** | cart-sidebar, shipping, stepper, multi-select, coupon, tax, delivery, py-address (15 total) | HIGH — most complex per-client variation |
| **@aiw/catalog** | category-layout, search, filters, brand-filter, hero-carousel, promo-carousel (10) | MEDIUM |
| **@aiw/account** | order-timeline, packing-slip, reorder, saved-card, notification-prefs, auth-wrapper (11) | MEDIUM |
| **@aiw/ui-extras** | skeleton, toast, tooltip, error-boundary, header, footer, dark-mode, mobile-nav (19) | MEDIUM — already partially covered by @aiw/ui |
| **@aiw/seo-extras** | json-ld, article-json-ld, faq-json-ld (3) | LOW — cookie-consent already extracted |
| **API routes** | 33 route files (checkout, products, auth, admin/*, db/*) | HIGH — each client needs its own DB, but middleware patterns could be shared |

---

**CI/CD gaps (32 repos not using ci-cd reusable workflows):**

- **Client sites** (15): bichos-gym, cocodrilo-fitness, depiflash, fun4me, luis-de-leon-concept, magnolia-peluqueria, mantra-spa, nudo, superspuma, etc. — zero workflows at all
- **Already have workflows but NOT using ci-cd repo** (5): elviajero, dayah-litworks, ozmontania, maiyu-atelier, villamayor-asociados, 3md — have standalone deploy.yml
- **Infra/tools**: paragu-ai-builder, aiw-infra, code-agent, code-agent-ui, solstein-v2, aiw-docs, telescope-ai, etc.

---

**Cross-cutting concerns found:**

| Area | Status | Issue |
|---|---|---|
| Monitoring | NONE | Only node-exporter running. No Prometheus/Grafana/Loki/Alertmanager |
| Backups | OK | Cron jobs for PG, volumes, Qdrant, configs — daily at 3am |
| Health checks | /30min cron | Script-based, not in a centralized dashboard |
| SSL | OK | Traefik auto-manages via Let's Encrypt ACME (no acme.json file visible — uses another backend) |
| Deploy config | MANUAL | Many client sites don't have Dockerfiles in their repos (they deploy from paragu-ai-builder's sites/ directory) |
| DNS | OK | CF proxied, paragu-ai-builder has the config |

---

**Highest-ROI next moves:**

1. **@aiw/payments** — extract the 4 payment processors + factory. These are pure logic (no UI), reusable everywhere, and currently buried inside elviajero/lib/payment/
2. **@aiw/api-helpers** — common middleware patterns: supabase client factory, rate limiting, error responses, auth gates, CORS. All 33 API routes copy-paste these.
3. **CI/CD rollout** — bulk-add workflows to the 15 client repos that have zero CI/CD
4. **Monitoring stack** — Prometheus + Grafana on the VPS (biggest operational blind spot — no alerting if a site goes down)

Want me to extract **@aiw/payments** and **@aiw/api-helpers** into the base repo now? Those two alone would eliminate ~40% of copy-paste across all client sites.

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, and deployment follow-through.", "tags": ["nextjs", "ecommerce", "admin", "audit", "tailwind", "layout", "sidebar", "supabase", "auth", "cloudflare", "org-restructuring", "package-extraction"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: ecommerce-codebase-audit description: "Audit, navigate, and iterate on a Next.js e-commerce project (admin panel + storefront). Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, and deployment follow-through." version: 1.4.0 tags: [nextjs, ecommerce, admin, audit, tailwind, layout, sidebar, supabase, auth, cloudflare, org-restructuring, package-extraction] --- # Ecommerce Codebase Audit Inspect and improve a Next.js e-commerce admin panel. Focus on layout consistency, theme application, page structure, image assets, and auth flow. ## Audit Checklist ### Layout Consistency - [ ] Every admin page wraps content in `AdminShell` from `@/components/admin/admin-layout` - [ ] `AdminShell` includes the sidebar navigation (responsive: hidden on mobile, fixed on desktop) - [ ] Sidebar uses the same links and active-state highlighting across all pages - [ ] All pages render inside `` not just individually ### Theme System The admin theme uses emerald/zinc on a dark background (#0a0a0b / #0f0f10): ```tsx // Tailwind classes to look for bg-[#0f0f10] // sidebar + card backgrounds bg-zinc-800/60 // borders text-emerald-400 // active text bg-emerald-500/10 // active background text-zinc-400 // inactive text hover:bg-zinc-800/40 // hover state ``` - [ ] All pages use the same color tokens (not hardcoded different colors) - [ ] Active page shows `bg-emerald-500/10 text-emerald-400` with indicator dot - [ ] Inactive pages use `text-zinc-400 hover:text-zinc-200 hover:bg-zinc-800/40` ### Page Structure Each admin page follows this pattern: ```tsx 'use client' import { AdminShell, useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout" import { useState, useEffect } from "react" export default function PageName() { const { authed } = useAdminAuth() if (!authed) return null return ( <>

Page Title

{/* content */} ) } ``` - [ ] Pages use `{ authed } = useAdminAuth()` for auth guard - [ ] Pages use `AdminShell` wrapper (already in the root layout) - [ ] Pages use `fetch("/api/admin/*")` for data (not direct Supabase client) - [ ] Data fetched via `/api/admin/` routes that use service_role key server-side ### AdminShell Component Located in `@/components/admin/admin-layout` (or wherever it's imported from). The AdminShell provides: - Sidebar navigation with 4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración - Mobile responsiveness (hidden sidebar on mobile, toggle with hamburger) - Auth guard via `useAdminAuth` - Consistent background and layout padding ## Auth Flow & Cookie Debugging ### The Core Trap: Two Auth Paths Crossing In Next.js + Supabase e-commerce projects, there are usually TWO auth paths that can conflict: **Path A — Consumer auth (useAuth):** - Login page calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` directly from the browser client - Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` browser client sets the session cookie automatically - `/mi-cuenta` reads session via `supabase.auth.getSession()` — works because cookie exists **Path B — Admin auth (useAdminAuth / /api/auth):** - Login page calls `/api/auth` with `action: "login"` - API route creates an SSR client with `createServerClient()`, but often has `setAll() {}` empty - **The session cookie never gets set on the response** — the API returns tokens, but no cookie - Admin layout falls back to localStorage or custom cookie **Common failure modes:** 1. **Login works (gets tokens) but /mi-cuenta shows unauthenticated** — consumer login page fetches `/api/auth` instead of calling Supabase directly. Fix: use `useAuth().login()` (Supabase browser client) which sets the correct session cookie. 2. **Login works but /admin redirects back to /login** — admin layout's `useAdminAuth()` calls `/api/auth?action=me` with a token from localStorage, but the API route's token verification path fails. Fix: ensure the API route can verify tokens against Supabase REST API (not just SSR cookies). ### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ### Debugging Auth Issues ```bash # Check if API route is reachable curl -sX POST https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/api/auth \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"action":"login","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","password":"test"}' | head -c 200 # Check if session cookie is set curl -sv https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/mi-cuenta 2>&1 | grep -i 'set-cookie' # Check server logs docker service logs elviajero-comercio_web --tail 30 --no-task-ids ``` ## Visual Asset Workflow ### Full Asset Overhaul Pattern When doing a complete visual assets redesign (inventory, generation, deployment): #### Phase 1: Inventory ```bash # List all files on disk find public/images -type f | sort # List all code references grep -rohP '"/images/[^"'"'"']+' --include='*.{tsx,ts,json,css}' | sed 's/^"//' | sort -u # Cross-reference: 3 outcomes per image # 1. Referenced + on disk = OK # 2. Referenced + missing = FIX (add file) # 3. On disk + no reference = ORPHAN (delete) # Size audit — small = programmatic (low quality) find public/images -name "*.png" -size -50k ``` #### Phase 2: Categorization | Type | Size threshold | Keep as | Upgrade? | |------|---------------|---------|----------| | SVGs | varies | Vector | Only if they're emoji text | | Real photos | >50KB | PNG | No | | Programmatic renders | <50KB | PNG | Yes — regenerate with richer Pillow composites | | Icons | 4-8KB | PNG | Intentionally small — minimalist silhouettes | | Hero backgrounds | <20KB | PNG | Yes — add atmosphere, silhouettes, gradients | #### Phase 3: Generation (no AI API available) Use Pillow for progressive enhancement rounds: **Round 1 — Placeholders:** ```python # Sky gradient + bokeh circles + category silhouettes # Write to a generate script, deploy, iterate ``` **Round 2 — Richer details:** - Tree silhouettes, stars, glowing tent, moon - River reflections, fishing rod, sun at horizon - Mountain trails, hiker with backpack - Headlight beams, road lines, car approaching - Dust clouds, motorcycle, fence - Fields, barn, sun, clouds **Round 3 — AI (if REPLICATE_API_TOKEN or equivalent available):** ```python if call_replicate(prompt, w, h, path): print("AI generated") else: render_fallback(path) # keep best Pillow version ``` **Round 4 — Self-critique:** After deploying, review each asset and write a brutal critique doc. Then regenerate the worst offenders in the next round. #### Phase 4: Deployment Pitfalls **Cloudflare cache (IMPORTANT):** - CF sets `cache-control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` — 1 YEAR cache - No CF API key? Only option is **rename the file** (logo.png → logo-v2.png) - Then update ALL code references to point to new filename - Verify with curl, not browser: `curl -sk URL/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B"` - Then tell the user to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) **Docker layer cache:** - If files exist locally but return 404 on deploy, the Docker COPY was cached - Fix: `docker build --no-cache` or touch a file in the COPY layer **Verify the right file is served:** ```bash # Check HTML references curl -sk https://example.com/ | grep -oP '(logo|favicon)-v2' # Check file directly curl -sk https://example.com/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B" ``` ### Key Project-Specific Patterns (El Viajero) - Product images: `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/products/*.png` (34 products) - Category backgrounds (dynamic SVG): `/images/categories/${slug}.svg` — slug values in `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` - Category heroes: heroImage prop → `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` → `/images/marketing/hero-*.png` - Hero carousel: content-driven array in `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/hero-carousel-*.png` - Testimonials: `"image"` field in `content/es.json` → `/images/testimonials/avatar-*.png` - Logo: `header.tsx` → `/images/logo.png` (or `logo-v2.png` after cache-bust) - Favicon: `layout.tsx` → `/images/favicon.png` (or `favicon-v2.png`) - OG image: `layout.tsx` → `/images/og-viajero.svg` - PWA icons: `manifest.json` → `/images/icon-192.png`, `/images/icon-512.png` ### Generation Recipes **Category icons (128x128):** Create a Python script with `ImageDraw` — tent, fish, umbrella, backpack, car, motorcycle, barn silhouettes on green circle background. These are intentionally small (4-8KB). **Hero banners (1920x600):** Create scene composites: 1. Sky gradient (top to bottom, warm/cool depending on scene) 2. Ground layer (dark rectangle) 3. Scene elements (tree silhouettes, stars, moon, tent, river, fishing rod, mountains, road, headlights, barn, fence, sun) 4. Bokeh overlay (random circles with low alpha) 5. Gaussian blur (radius 0.3-0.5) for polish **Testimonial avatars (200x200):** If no AI available, create colored circles with basic face features (eyes, smile, hair shape, shirt). Tell the client these are placeholders for real customer photos. **PWA icons:** Render from the logo at 192x192 and 512x512 with anti-aliasing. ## Multi-Locale Content Updates (PAB Sites) When updating content across all locales (en/es/nl/de) in a PAB (Paragu-AI Builder) project: ### Safe approach vs. pitfalls 1. **Python JSON manipulation is safer than `patch` tool for large content changes.** The `patch` tool works well for small string replacements, but when replacing JSON blocks spanning 50+ lines, it can match the wrong text, leave orphaned content, and produce valid-but-wrong JSON. This happened when replacing the `processPage` section — patch matched a subset, leaving trailing orphan that was syntactically valid but structurally wrong. 2. **When `patch` goes wrong: stop, don't stack.** If the first patch produces unexpected results (orphan content, duplicated sections, lost keys), do NOT apply more patches on top. Switch immediately to Python `json.load()/json.dump()` — read the broken file, fix the data structure in memory, and write it back clean. Stacking patches compounds the mistakes and makes the file increasingly fragile to further edits. The `processPage` fix required three patches to clean up what one wrong match did. 3. **`patch` can match content from a previously-applied patch** — If a file was already modified, the old_string might not match what's actually in the file. Always read the file fresh before patching, and verify with a direct key inspection after (`python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('f')); print(d.get('targetKey',{}).keys())"`). 4. **Shell variable expansion fails in `execute_code`'s multi-line Python strings** — `for f in $FILES; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))"; done` breaks because `$f` isn't expanded inside the Python string. Use explicit file paths, a temp script, or Python's own `glob` module instead. 5. **Always validate JSON structure after content changes, not just syntax.** A file can be valid JSON but structurally wrong (missing keys, wrong step counts, orphan content). After validation: ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file'))" # syntax check ``` Also verify structural integrity: ```python d["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] # should have 5 steps d["home"]["services"]["groups"] # should have 3 groups d["aboutPage"]["story"]["paragraphs"] # should have 3 paragraphs ``` 6. **When deleting orphaned content from a botched patch, read the file with JSON parser first**, then find the structural key that contains the orphan data. Delete it from the Python dict and re-write. Don't try to surgically remove lines with patch. 7. **Process step consistency rule:** The same process narrative lives in 6 places across the site. Update ALL of these together in the same script block — missing any one creates inconsistency: - `home.process.steps` (homepage) - `processPage.process.steps` (process page) - `landingInversor.process.steps`, `landingTrust.process.steps`, `landingLifestyle.process.steps`, `landingEmpresa.process.steps` (LinkedIn ad landing pages) 8. **Process for adding new content sections across locales:** - Step 1: Define the content data structure in a Python dict with all localizations - Step 2: Load each locale file, apply transformations, write back - Step 3: Validate JSON syntax for all files - Step 4: Verify structural keys match expectations (count groups, steps, paragraphs) ```python for lang in ["en", "es", "nl", "de"]: with open(f"content/{lang}.json") as f: d = json.load(f) d["parentSection"]["newKey"] = translations[lang] with open(f"content/{lang}.json", "w") as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` 9. **Section structure for the 'Our Services' pattern:** ```python data["home"]["services"] = { "eyebrow": "Our Services", "title": "Section title", "groups": [ {"id": "group-id", "title": "Group Name", "subtitle": "Group subtitle.", "items": [{"title": "Service", "description": "Desc.", "icon": "IconName"}]} ] } ``` 10. **Section structure for the 'Story' pattern:** ```python data["aboutPage"]["story"] = { "title": "Our Story", "eyebrow": "Tagline", "paragraphs": ["Para 1.", "Para 2.", "Para 3."] } ``` 11. **Section structure for the 'Process steps' pattern:** ```python data["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] = [ {"number": 1, "title": "Step Name", "description": "Long description.", "icon": "IconName", "image": {"$img": "process.key"}, "duration": "Week 1"} ] ``` 12. **Landing page Calendly URL pattern:** ```python base = "https://calendly.com/nexaparaguay/consulta" utm = f"utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={campaign}" url = f"{base}?{utm}&utm_content=cta-{placement}-{lang}" ``` ## Deployment ### Docker Deployment After modifying content assets (images, content JSON, or static files): 1. **Rebuild** — `npm run build` catches missing images and type errors 2. **Rebuild Docker image** — `docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .` 3. **Deploy** — `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false` 4. **Verify** — `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/ | head -5` 5. **Force rolling update** — if same image tag, `docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web` Never assume changes to static content or images are live — Docker rebuild and stack deploy are required. Browser cache is NOT the server — always curl first. ### Production Monitoring After every deploy, add: - Healthcheck endpoint at `/api/health` returning `{status, timestamp, uptime}` - Docker HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile (every 30s, 40s start grace, 3 retries) - Cron healthcheck via Hermes: every 15m, auto-redeploy on failure - Shell check script: `/root/scripts/check-{project}.sh` ### Docker Build Timeout `docker build` with npm install can take >120s on cold cache. Set terminal timeout to 300s for builds. If it times out, retry — the npm cache is warm on the second run and it will complete faster. #### Next.js `pages/` Folder Conflict (v16+) Next.js 16+ errors with "`pages` and `app` directories should be under the same folder" when a `pages/` data directory exists at project root alongside `src/app/`. Fix: 1. Rename `pages/` to `nexa-pages/` 2. Update all `page.tsx` references from `'pages'` to `'nexa-pages'` 3. Update Dockerfile to copy `nexa-pages/` into the standalone output 4. Run `docker build --no-cache` after rename (Docker layer cache may use old path) #### Docker Standalone + Data Files Next.js `output: "standalone"` produces a `server.js` that does `process.chdir(__dirname)` — so `process.cwd()` points to `.next/standalone/`, not the app root. Data files read at runtime via `readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), ...))` must be copied into `.next/standalone/`: ```dockerfile COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./.next/standalone/nexa-pages COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./.next/standalone/content ``` Without this, the container runs fine but returns static HTML with fallback data because `readFileSync` silently fails and the catch block returns defaults. #### The "Welcome" Bug (Static SSG Fallback) When a PAB page always renders "Welcome" regardless of content changes, the root cause is in the page component's fallback chain: ```tsx // BROKEN: home.json has 'titleKey' not 'title' — always falls through {homePage.title || content?.title || 'Welcome'} // FIXED: use siteName from the locale JSON {content?.siteName || 'Nexa Paraguay'} ``` Check that `getPages()` and `getContent()` actually read files via `readFileSync`/`readdirSync` at build time. The `catch` block silently swallowing the error is the symptom — verify the files are accessible during the Docker build stage. ## Communication Style (Ivan/ParaguAI) This user gives short commands and wants short responses. Key rules: - **No walls of text.** Deliver analysis in tables/bullets, not paragraphs. - **No explanations of what you're about to do.** Just do it and report what changed. - **Batch operations always.** Never one file at a time for repetitive work. - **One recommendation, not options.** Do not list alternatives unless asked. - **Final output: what changed, then stop.** No "Would you like me to...?" follow-ups. - **Deploy automatically.** If you changed something, build + deploy + verify without being asked. - **Hard refresh solves browser cache.** When user says "I still see old images", confirm server serves new file (curl), then tell them to hard refresh. Do NOT re-deploy. - **Phrasing: "Done." + summary + nothing else.** Test: if your response has more than 5 items of information after "Done.", it's too long. ## Full-Site Audit Methodology (PAB Static Sites) When performing a full site content audit on a PAB multi-locale site like nexa-paraguay, follow this 20-point hierarchy. Each finding gets: status emoji (✅/🟡/🔴), priority label, and actionable next step. [...existing 20 points...] ## Post-Audit: Repo Enhancement Research (Optional) After the audit identifies gaps, consider running `repo-enhancement-research` on the target repo to discover: - Alternative frameworks/libraries that do things better - High-star templates with patterns to steal (admin dashboards, auth flows, checkout) - Dependency upgrades (better-sqlite3 → supabase, framer-motion → CSS animations, etc.) ```bash python3 /root/.hermes/skills/software-development/repo-enhancement-research/scripts/search.py /path/to/repo ``` This is most valuable when the audit reveals: stale dependencies, missing features competitors have, or admin panel gaps. It is NOT needed for purely content/skin changes. ## Cross-Repo Standard & Base Package Architecture When working across multiple client repos in the same org (Ai-Whisperers), the client repos share no code. The most advanced repo (typically elviajero-comercio) has the richest feature set. The best practice is: ### Step 1: Feature inventory Compare each repo's feature set against elviajero's reference standard: - 104+ components vs <10 - 18 admin pages vs 0 - auth (Supabase SSR + OAuth) vs nothing - 4 payment gateways vs 0 - cart/checkout vs nothing - 3 locales vs 0 - shadcn/ui vs bare tailwind - Docker multi-stage + healthcheck vs basic - PWA + manifest vs nothing - SEO (JSON-LD, sitemap, RSS) vs nothing ### Step 2: Extract shared packages Create a base repo (`@ai-whisperers/base`) that publishes npm packages: ```bash packages/ ui/ → @ai-whisperers/ui (Button, Card, Dialog, DataTable, Toast, etc.) commerce/ → @ai-whisperers/commerce (cart, checkout, payment gateways, products) admin/ → @ai-whisperers/admin (AdminLayout, StatCard, charts, ImageUpload) auth/ → @ai-whisperers/auth (AuthProvider, OAuth, middleware, Supabase SSR) whatsapp/ → @ai-whisperers/whatsapp (WhatsAppFloat, share, templates) seo/ → @ai-whisperers/seo (JSON-LD, OG, Analytics, CookieConsent) i18n/ → @ai-whisperers/i18n (locales, language switcher, currency) theme/ → @ai-whisperers/theme (Tailwind preset, tokens, fonts) ``` ### Step 3: Client repo migration Each client repo installs `@aiw/*` packages and replaces local copies: ```json { "dependencies": { "@ai-whisperers/ui": "^1.0.0", "@ai-whisperers/auth": "^1.0.0", "@ai-whisperers/commerce": "^1.0.0" } } ``` ### Step 4: Client-specific code stays Each client keeps: content JSON, images, page layouts, business-logic pages (about, contact, blog, FAQ, terms), Dockerfile, docker-compose, config files. ### Tool Usage Pitfall: read_file + write_file Corrupts Content **CRITICAL BUG:** The `read_file` tool returns content with `LINE_NUM|` prefixes (e.g., `1|"use client"\n2|import ...`). If you pass this content directly to `write_file`, the line numbers are written into the file, corrupting it. **Symptoms:** - TS errors: `TS1109: Expression expected` at every line - Node.js `require()` fails with unexpected tokens - `head -5` shows `1| "use client"` (the `1|` is literal) **Prevention:** 1. For simple reads, use `terminal(f"cat {path}")` instead of `read_file` when the content will be re-written 2. OR strip the prefix before writing: ```python import re content = re.sub(r'^\s*\d+\|', '', content, flags=re.MULTILINE) ``` 3. For bulk operations, use a cleanup script after any batch write: ```python def clean_file(path): with open(path) as f: content = f.read() cleaned = re.sub(r'^\s*\d+\|', '', content, flags=re.MULTILINE) if cleaned != content: with open(path, 'w') as f: f.write(cleaned) return True return False ``` ### Client Dependency Strategy: file: -> github: -> npm When client installs `@ai-whisperers/*` packages, dependency strategy depends on context: | Context | Strategy | Path Pattern | |---------|----------|-------------| | Local dev | `file:` (relative) | `"file:../base/packages/ui"` | | CI/CD build | `github:` tag | `"github:Ai-Whisperers/base#v0.1.0"` | | External | npm (GitHub Packages) | `"^0.1.0"` with `.npmrc` | **Secret scanning trap:** `file:/root/...` paths trigger GitHub push protection ("Push cannot contain secrets"). Use relative `file:../` or `github:` URLs before pushing to repos with branch protection. ### Package API Naming Mismatches When elviajero's internal functions differ from the package exports: | elviajero (old) | @ai-whisperers/* (new) | Why | |-----------------|----------------------|-----| | `getAdminClient()` | `createAdminClient()` | Packages use factory pattern | | `CartItem.imageUrl` | `CartItem.image` | Cleaner field name | | `Order.created_at` | `Order.createdAt` | camelCase convention | | `middleware.ts: updateSession()` | Inline in middleware | Avoid Next.js instance collision | Fix: global sed for batch renames, then individual patches for type mismatches. ### Type Compatibility: snake_case <-> camelCase Supabase returns snake_case (`created_at`, `customer_name`). Package types use camelCase (`createdAt`, `customerName`). **Best fix:** Add both shapes and an index signature: ```typescript export interface Order { id: string createdAt?: string // camelCase (package convention) customerName?: string created_at?: string // snake_case (Supabase) customer_name?: string [key: string]: any // allow any field } ``` ### next/headers cookies() Async (Next.js 15+) In Next.js 15, `cookies()` and `headers()` return Promises: ```typescript // OLD: const cookieStore = cookies() cookieStore.getAll() // NEW (Next.js 15): const cookieStore = await cookies() cookieStore.getAll() ``` Server-side Supabase client callbacks must be async: ```typescript cookies: { async getAll() { const store = await cookieStore; return store.getAll() }, async setAll(cookiesToSet) { const store = await cookieStore; /* ... */ } } ``` ### Package Extraction: Rewriting @/ Imports When extracting components from a Next.js app into standalone npm packages, three import patterns need fixing: 1. **`@/content/es.json`** — This is an app-specific content file. Replace with prop-based config or remove entirely. The package should NOT depend on this file. ```tsx // BEFORE: import content from "@/content/es.json" // AFTER: the component accepts a config prop or provides defaults ``` 2. **`@/lib/*`** — App-specific utility imports. Either: - Rewrite to relative imports if the utility is extracted into the same package - Replace with a stub/comment if the utility should come from the consumer app - Import from another `@ai-whisperers/*` package if the utility lives there 3. **`../../lib/cart-context`** style paths — Files extracted into subdirectories (e.g., `cart/`) may have relative paths that point outside the package. Fix by: - Creating the target files within the package (e.g., `src/types.ts`, `src/cart-context.tsx`) - Correcting the relative paths to point within `src/` **Verification:** After fixing imports, run `npx tsc --noEmit` in the package to catch all remaining issues in one pass, then fix them all before rebuilding. ### Step 5: Template update Update `template-nextjs-client` to pre-install all `@aiw/*` packages so new client sites start with the full feature set. ## Full-Site Audit Methodology (PAB Static Sites)** — nav-linked vs orphan pages 2. **SEO** — titles, descriptions, schema markup (LocalBusiness, Service, FAQ, Article) 3. **Content completeness** — locale comparison, missing keys, translation quality flags 4. **Services** — structure, dedicated page, nav links, consistency with questionnaire 5. **Story/About** — founder narrative, team info, personal touch 6. **Process steps** — consistency across all pages that display process (home, process page, landing pages) 7. **Landing pages** — LinkedIn ad pages, Calendly URL consistency, UTM params 8. **Pricing** — packages vs a-la-carte, questionnaire alignment 9. **Testimonials** — real client names vs AI-generated, consent status 10. **Contact info** — phone, WhatsApp, email, address, hours, Calendly, booking URL 11. **Footer** — columns, links, social profiles, missing links 12. **Placeholders & TODOs** — categorize by severity (🔴 blocks launch, 🟡 quality, 🟢 nice-to-have) 13. **Analytics & integrations** — GA4, HubSpot, Mailchimp real vs placeholder IDs 14. **Deployment** — Docker, Traefik, CDN, domain, staging 15. **Image assets** — webp/png coverage, placeholder status, alt text quality 16. **Brand tokens** — colors, typography, logo variants 17. **Compliance** — GDPR, AML, SEPRELAD, privacy policy, license numbers 18. **Conversion flow** — user journey from landing to booking, post-booking follow-up 19. **Content questionnaire alignment** — cross-reference every phrase from the client's response against current site 20. **Content questionnaire cross-reference** — after making content changes based on client responses, run a final phrase-level check: extract every substantive claim/description from the client's written response and verify it exists verbatim (or semantically equivalent) in the current site content. Use Python `json.dumps(d).lower()` and check for key terms. Count misses. Log any semantic vs verbatim gaps. Do NOT rely on patch-tool verification alone — a file read by python and checked programmatically catches things eyeballing diffs misses. 21. **Repository hygiene** — commit granularity, file organization, unnecessary files Each finding gets: status emoji (✅/🟡/🔴), priority label, and actionable next step.", "path": "software-development/ecommerce-codebase-audit/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/software-development/ecommerce-codebase-audit", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/docker-env-var-verification.md", "references/nexa-paraguay-may5-session.md", "references/el-viajero-auth-flow.md", "references/nexa-paraguay-session.md", "references/visual-asset-audit-workflow.md", "references/nexa-paraguay-content-update.md", "references/el-viajero-admin-panel.md", "references/supabase-debugging-patterns.md"], "scripts": ["scripts/generate-placeholder-pipeline.py"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available"}

# Org Architecture Planning

Design and execute a repo restructuring for a multi-repo GitHub organization.
Covers org audit, monorepo design, centralized CI/CD, shared test infra, and cron diagnostics.

## Workflow

### Step 1: Full Org Inventory

```bash
# Full repo list with all relevant fields
gh repo list ORG --limit 200 --json name,description,isFork,isPrivate,visibility,primaryLanguage,pushedAt,createdAt,isArchived,repositoryTopics
```

For each repo, also fetch languages:
```bash
gh api "repos/ORG/REPO/languages"
```

### Best Practice: Distinguish Personal Account from Org

Check both:
```bash
gh api /orgs/ORG-NAME  # 404 if it's a personal account, not an org
gh api /user --jq '{login,type,id}'  # 'User' vs 'Organization'
```

Personal accounts don't have org-level settings (teams, secrets, webhooks). Adjust expectations.

### Step 1b: Fetch All Org Config in One Pass

```bash
# Team info
gh api /orgs/ORG/teams --jq '.[] | {name,slug,privacy,description}'

# Members  
gh api /orgs/ORG/members --jq '.[].login'

# Org secrets (often 0)
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/secrets --jq '.total_count as $c | .secrets[] | {name,visibility}'

# Actions permissions / security posture
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/permissions --jq '{enabled_repositories,allowed_actions,sha_pinning_required}'
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/permissions/workflow --jq '{default_workflow_permissions,can_approve_pull_request_reviews}'

# Webhooks
gh api /orgs/ORG/hooks --jq '.[] | {id,type,config:{url,content_type},events,active}'

# Runners
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/runners --jq '.runners[] | {name,os,status,busy,labels:[.labels[].name]}'
```

### Step 1c: Enumerate All Environments + Branch Protection

```bash
# Environments across repos
for repo in repo1 repo2 repo3; do
  gh api "repos/ORG/$repo/environments" --jq '.environments[].name'
done

# Branch protection
gh api "repos/ORG/$repo/branches/main/protection" --jq '{required_status_checks,enforce_admins,required_pull_request_reviews,restrictions}'
```

### Step 2: Categorize Repos

Group into these categories:

| Category | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **core-platform** | Builder engines, monorepos | paragu-ai-builder, paragu-ai |
| **client-sites** | Production websites | elviajero, nexa, golden-visa |
| **templates** | Starter repos, shared components | template-nextjs-client, client-kit |
| **infra-tools** | DevOps, CI/CD, agents | priorities, code-agent, aiw-infra |
| **research** | Data pipelines, experiments | ma-research-pipeline, solstein |
| **docs** | Documentation repos | aiw-docs |
| **leads** | Auto-generated, experimental | magnolia-peluqueria, mantra-spa |
| **other** | Stale/archived/unrelated | energy21, telescope-ai |

### Step 3: Decide Action Per Repo

**ARCHIVE when:**
- Description says "[ARCHIVED]" or "Superseded by..."
- Zero disk usage (empty repos)
- Auto-generated with no updates (leads with 104KB identical size)
- Stale >30 days with no foreseeable work
- Content was migrated to another repo

**MERGE when:**
- Duplicate purpose (client-kit → template-nextjs-client)
- Same brand/product (nexa-propiedades → nexa-paraguay)
- Monorepo + standalone builder (paragu-ai → paragu-ai-builder)

**SPLIT when:**
- Monolith >100MB with multiple subsystems
- paragu-ai-builder pattern: engine + admin + builder packages

**FIX when:**
- Monorepo without workspaces (paragu-ai has only `typescript` as dep)
- Stale template (template-nextjs-client missing shadcn/ui, supabase, auth)

**KEEP when:**
- Active client site with regular updates
- Core infrastructure
- Research with ongoing work
- Public documentation

### Step 4: Design Monorepo Architecture

When the user wants "1 base repo for all client sites":

```
org-base/
├── packages/
│   ├── ui/              # shadcn/ui components (Button, Card, Dialog, DataTable, etc.)
│   ├── commerce/        # Cart, checkout, payment gateways, product cards
│   ├── admin/           # Admin layout, DataTable, stat cards, charts
│   ├── auth/            # Supabase auth (SSR, OAuth, admin role check)
│   ├── whatsapp/        # WhatsApp Float, templates, messaging
│   ├── seo/             # JSON-LD, sitemap, OG, analytics
│   ├── i18n/            # Translation helpers + locale management
│   ├── config/          # Tailwind theme, design tokens, fonts
│   ├── test-vitest/     # @org/test-vitest — shared vitest config, mocks, factories
│   ├── test-python/     # @org/test-python — shared pytest config + fixtures
│   └── test-e2e/        # @org/test-e2e — shared Playwright config + page objects
├── apps/
│   ├── template/        # Reference template (uses packages/*)
│   ├── elviajero/       # Client site (uses packages/*)
│   ├── golden-visa/     # Client site (uses packages/*)
│   ├── nexa/            # Client site (uses packages/*)
│   └── builder/         # Existing builder (moves here)
├── package.json         # npm workspaces
└── tsconfig.json        # Shared TS config
```

**Why monorepo (not separate packages):**
- Single `npm install` for all clients
- Types shared between packages
- Synchronized versions — one change reflects everywhere
- One CI/CD pipeline

### Step 5: Extract Shared Packages

Identify cross-repo duplicated code:

| Package | Source (most mature version) | Contains |
|---------|------------------------------|----------|
| `@org/ui` | builder UI + shadcn | Button, Card, Dialog, Toast, Skeleton, Badge, Tooltip, forms |
| `@org/commerce` | elviajero | CartSidebar, CheckoutStepper, ProductCard, PriceDisplay, AddToCart |
| `@org/admin` | elviajero | AdminLayout, StatCard, DataTable, ChartCard, ImageUpload |
| `@org/auth` | elviajero | AuthProvider, AuthGuard, login/register, OAuth |
| `@org/whatsapp` | builder + elviajero | WhatsAppFloat, share, message templates |
| `@org/seo` | builder | JsonLd, OG, sitemap, Analytics, CookieConsent |
| `@org/test-vitest` | base monorepo | defineVitestConfig, jest-dom setup, mocks, factories |
| `@org/test-python` | base monorepo | pytest conftest, data factories, assertions |
| `@org/test-e2e` | base monorepo | Playwright config, page objects |

El Viajero is always the best extraction source because it has the most mature implementations (86 components, 18 admin pages, full auth + payment + checkout).

### Step 6: Migration Order (Cold Turkey vs Gradual)

**Phase 1 — Create monorepo:**
```bash
mkdir org-base && cd org-base
npm init -w packages/ui -w packages/commerce -w packages/admin
git init
```

**Phase 2 — Extract from elviajero (first client):**
- Copy ui components to `packages/ui`
- Extract commerce components to `packages/commerce`
- Extract admin to `packages/admin`
- Extract auth to `packages/auth`
- Update elviajero imports from `@/components/` to `@org/ui`, `@org/commerce`, etc.

**Phase 3 — Migrate other sites gradually**
- Each site becomes an app, imports from packages
- Old repos archived after migration

### Step 7: Clean Up

After migration:
```bash
# Archive old repos
gh repo edit ORG/REPO --visibility private  # if public
gh api -X PATCH repos/ORG/REPO -f archived=true
```

## Pitfalls

- **Don't archive before migration.** Only archive after the new repo is live and deployed.
- **Check `diskUsage`** — repos with 0KB are truly empty and safe to archive immediately.
- **Auto-generated leads** all have identical size (~104KB) — they're clones. Never touch, just archive.
- **Public repos need private first** before archive (or leave public if docs/reference).
- **Monorepo with no workspaces** (paragu-ai) is broken by design — fix or delete, don't keep as-is.
- **Size check matters** — paragu-ai-builder at 479MB is the largest monolith. Splitting it is weeks of work, not hours.
- **Client sites don't all need to migrate** — 10-20 small static sites (<1MB each) are fine standalone. Only the complex ones (ecommerce, admin, auth) benefit from shared packages.
- **Archive in batches, not one-by-one.** Group all stale repos into a single commit/PR.
- **No branch protection is the norm** for early-stage orgs. Document it as a gap, don't fix it without user request.
- **Workflow files are the best signal for CI maturity.** Count workflows per repo. A repo with 8 workflows (like paragu-ai-builder) is the reference standard. Repos with 0 workflows are un-automated.
- **RepositoryTopics field returns null for org repos queried via `gh repo list`**. Use `gh api` endpoint directly.
- **Personal repos that became org repos** may have stale personal copies. Cross-reference both accounts.
- **When analyzing, use `gh api` not `curl`** to avoid auth token management. The `gh` CLI handles token injection automatically.
- **Workflow YAML decoding**: `gh api repos/ORG/REPO/contents/.github/workflows/NAME --jq .content | base64 -d`
- **Contribution stats (last 90 days) return empty `[]`** for org repos owned by automation. The stats endpoint only tracks human-committed changes; AI/agent commits don't count. Don't infer "dead repo" from this.
- **Reusable workflows require a specific ref** — pin to `@main` or `@v1`. Using `@main` means every ci-cd push affects all clients. Using `@v1` requires tagging.
- **In `secrets: inherit` the called workflow can still access only secrets a direct push to the called repo can** — org secrets need visibility set to `all` or the specific repos.

## Package Extraction

### Copy Strategy — Battle-Tested

**NEVER use `read_file()['content']` → `write_file().`** The `read_file` tool returns `LINE_NUM|content` prefixes. Writing this back corrupts every file.

**✅ Safe approaches (in order of preference):**

1. **`terminal(f"cp {abs_src} {abs_dst}")`** — fastest, no corruption risk. Then `sed -i` for import rewrites.

2. **`terminal(f"cat {path}")['output']`** — for single-file content you need to modify before writing:
```python
raw = terminal(f"cat {source}/src/file.ts")['output']
raw = raw.replace("@/lib/utils", "@ai-whisperers/ui/cn")
write_file(dest, raw)
```

3. **Bulk fix corrupted files** (if you already used read_file→write_file):
```bash
python3 -c "
import os, re
for r,d,f in os.walk('.'):
  for fn in f:
    if fn.endswith(('.ts','.tsx','.js')):
      p=os.path.join(r,fn); c=open(p).read(); c2=re.sub(r'^\\s*\\d+\\|','',c,flags=re.MULTILINE)
      if c2!=c: open(p,'w').write(c2)
"
```

**❌ NEVER:**
```python
content = read_file(source)['content']  # has line numbers!
write_file(dest, content)               # corrupts file
```

### Batch Import Replacement Pattern (For User Who Wants "Ultrawork")

When migrating a client with dozens of files and the user says "ultrawork" or "just do it", do ALL replacements in one pipeline:

```bash
# 1. Install deps ONCE
while IFS='=' read -r pkg path; do
  npm install "$pkg@file:$path"
done < aiw-packages.txt

# 2. Batch sed ALL imports (specific patterns first, then catch-all)
find . -path ./node_modules -prune -o \( -name '*.ts' -o -name '*.tsx' \) -print | \
  xargs sed -i \
    -e 's|@/lib/supabase/|@ai-whisperers/auth/supabase/|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/auth-context|@ai-whisperers/auth/auth-context|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/storage-keys|@ai-whisperers/auth/storage-keys|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/cart-context|@ai-whisperers/commerce/cart/cart-context|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/currency|@ai-whisperers/commerce|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/wishlist|@ai-whisperers/commerce|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/reviews|@ai-whisperers/commerce|g' \
    -e 's|@/lib/utils|@ai-whisperers/ui/cn|g'

# 3. Delete redundant source files
rm -rf lib/auth lib/supabase components/ui components/admin
rm -f lib/storage-keys.ts lib/currency.ts lib/utils.ts components/analytics.tsx components/cookie-consent.tsx components/whatsapp-float.tsx

# 4. Build to verify
npm run build
```

3. **Run `npm run build` after each major batch** to catch errors immediately. Fix one layer at a time.

4. **For `file:` paths that trigger secret scanning**, use relative `file:../` paths instead of absolute `file:/root/...` paths in package.json before committing.

### Package Scaffolding Pattern

Each package needs:
- `package.json` (name, version, private: true/false, type: module, main/exports, files, scripts, deps)
- `tsconfig.json` (extends base, jsx: react-jsx)
- `src/index.ts` (re-exports)
- `src/*.tsx` components

Package.json template:
```json
{
  "name": "@org/pkg-name",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "private": false,
  "type": "module",
  "main": "./dist/index.js",
  "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
  "exports": { ".": { "import": "./dist/index.js", "types": "./dist/index.d.ts" } },
  "scripts": { "build": "tsc", "dev": "tsc --watch", "clean": "rm -rf dist" },
  "peerDependencies": { "next": "^15.0.0", "react": "^19.0.0" }
}
```

### Test Package Export Pattern

Test packages need many subpath exports for deep imports like `@org/test-vitest/factories/product`:

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": { "types": "./dist/index.d.ts", "import": "./dist/index.mjs", "require": "./dist/index.js" },
    "./config": { "types": "./dist/config.d.ts", "import": "./dist/config.mjs", "require": "./dist/config.js" },
    "./setup": { ... },
    "./mocks/supabase": { ... },
    "./mocks/next": { ... },
    "./mocks/commerce": { ... },
    "./factories": { ... },
    "./factories/product": { ... },
    "./factories/user": { ... },
    "./factories/order": { ... },
    "./factories/payment": { ... }
  }
}
```

### Import Rewriting Patterns

| Source Pattern | Package Pattern |
|---|---|
| `@/content/es.json` | Remove entirely; make component config/prop-driven |
| `@/lib/utils` | `./cn` (inline a cn utility) |
| `@/components/` | @org/ui or relative ./ |
| `@/lib/supabase/` | @org/auth/supabase/ |
| `@/lib/auth/` | @org/auth/ |

### Component Coupling Triage

After copying components, some will be **too tightly coupled** to the source app to extract cleanly in one pass. Common blockers:

| Blocker | Signal | Action |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Static content imports | `import content from "@/content/es.json"` | Comment out the import, make the component accept a `content` prop or config interface. If the component has 5+ content references spread across rendering, **remove from build** and flag for refactoring. |
| App-specific types from `@/types` | `import { Order } from "@/types/order"` | Create a minimal type stub in the package, or remove from build if the type is complex. |
| App-specific analytics/hooks | `import { useCart } from "@/lib/cart"` | Remove from build — these need a consumer-provided context/interface. |
| References to sibling components not extracted | `import { CartEmptyState } from "./cart-empty-state"` | Extract the sibling too, or remove both. |

**Triage workflow (run after copy + import fix attempt):**

```bash
# 1. Find all unfixable imports
grep -rn "@/\|@/content\|@/lib\|@/types" packages/pkg/src/ 2>/dev/null

# 2. For each file with unfixable imports, check the coupling depth
# If the file references 5+ app-specific modules, remove from build:
rm -f packages/pkg/src/problematic-file.tsx
# And comment out the export in index.ts

# 3. Try building just the package
cd packages/pkg && npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1

# 4. Iterate: remove any remaining failing files that require
# consumer-provided types, content, or context. The rule is:
# a package component must work standalone with only React + consumed peer deps.
```

**When to remove from build vs. when to refactor:**

- Remove if the component references content files, app state, or types that don't exist in any other client project. These are **app-specific** components that only make sense in their original context.
- Refactor if the component is universally useful (cart, product card, checkout) but has app-specific imports. Extract the imports into props/interfaces.
- **Flag removed components** in a `docs/pending-extractions.md` file in the package so future sessions know what's missing.

**After triage, the package should build with zero errors.** A package that doesn't build is unusable by consumers. It's better to ship a smaller set of clean, buildable components than a large set of broken ones.

### Subpath Exports (required for deep imports)

Each package that exports submodules needs explicit `exports` in package.json:

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": { "import": "./dist/index.js", "types": "./dist/index.d.ts" },
    "./supabase/browser": { "import": "./dist/supabase/browser.js", "types": "./dist/supabase/browser.d.ts" },
    "./supabase/server": { "import": "./dist/supabase/server.js", "types": "./dist/supabase/server.d.ts" }
  }
}
```

TypeScript won't resolve `@org/auth/supabase/browser` without these entries.

### Common Errors

- **read_file line-number corruption**: The read_file tool returns content prefixed with LINE_NUM|. Never pipe output directly to write_file. Use terminal(cat path) for content you rewrite, or strip with re.sub(r'^\s*\d+\|', '', content, flags=re.MULTILINE).
- **@/content/es.json imports**: Build-time static imports. Replace with prop interfaces or remove entirely.
- **.ts extension in imports**: shadcn/ui files import ./cn.ts. TypeScript forbids this without allowImportingTsExtensions. Remove the .ts suffix.
- **Duplicate exports**: When auth-context and supabase/browser both export createClient, remove one re-export from index.ts.
- **next/headers cookies() API**: Next.js 15 made cookies() async. Must await before calling methods.
- **Missing sidebar reference**: Admin packages reference ./sidebar which may not exist in the source. Create a minimal sidebar or check if the component exists first.
- **Node modules in commits**: Always ensure .gitignore covers node_modules/, .turbo/, dist/, packages/*/node_modules/ before first commit.
- **tsup DTS build fails** due to version conflicts between rolldown and rollup in the dependency tree. Skip `--dts` flag for test packages; they're private and consumers get types from the source .ts files directly.
- **next/headers cookies() is async in Next.js 15+**: Must `await` before calling `.getAll()`/`.setAll()`:
  ```typescript
  const cookieStore = await cookies()  // NOT: const cookieStore = cookies()
  ```
- **Middleware + `file:` linking causes NextRequest type collision**: When a client repo uses `file:` deps for `@ai-whisperers/auth`, importing `@ai-whisperers/auth/supabase/middleware` creates two separate `next` instances. The middleware's `NextRequest` parameter becomes incompatible with the package's `NextRequest`. Fix: **inline the middleware** — copy the `updateSession` logic directly into `middleware.ts` instead of importing from the package. The middleware is small enough that duplication is fine.
- **Secret scanning blocks pushes with `file:/root/...` paths** — GitHub detects patterns that look like secrets. Use relative `file:../` paths or switch to `github:` URLs before pushing to repos with push protection enabled.
- **Removing branch protection** to force-push a clean history: `gh api repos/Org/repo/branches/main/protection --method DELETE`. Re-enable afterward with a minimal config or leave unprotected (document the gap).
- **Secret in git history fix**: `git checkout --orphan clean-branch; git rm -rf .; git checkout main -- desired-files; git branch -D main; git branch -m main; git push origin main --force`
- **The `read_file` tool returns `LINE_NUM|` prefixed content.** Never pipe output directly to `write_file`. See `references/package-extraction-error-transcripts.md` for details and recovery commands.

### Quick-start: The ci-cd Repo Pattern

Create a `ci-cd` repo with reusable workflows. Each client repo gets a single caller file:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
jobs:
  ci:
    uses: Org/ci-cd/.github/workflows/ci-nextjs.yml@main
    with:
      site-url: 'https://client.paragu-ai.com'
    secrets: inherit
  deploy:
    needs: ci
    uses: Org/ci-cd/.github/workflows/deploy-vps.yml@main
    with:
      site-name: 'client-name'
    secrets: inherit
```

The ci-cd repo also contains VPS deploy scripts (installed to `/opt/stacks/ci-cd/scripts/`), templates, and a `clients.json` registry.

## Cron Job Diagnostics

### Finding the Issue

1. Check error logs:
   ```bash
   grep -i "cron\|error\|fail\|openrouter\|rate\|quota\|timeout" ~/.hermes/logs/errors.log
   grep "cron.*error\|job.*failed" ~/.hermes/logs/agent.log
   ```

2. Common errors seen in production:
   - **`No LLM provider configured`** — config lost api_key after update. Key exists in `.env` but `config.yaml` has `api_key: null`. Fix: Write the key from `.env` into `config.yaml`.
   - **`401 - User not found`** — API key expired or invalid.
   - **`Connection error. All connection attempts failed`** — network issue (transient, VPS connectivity).

### Counting and Examining Jobs

```python
import json
with open("/root/.hermes/cron/jobs.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)
jobs = data.get("jobs", [])
print(f"Total: {len(jobs)}")
for j in jobs:
    print(f"{j['name']:25s} state={j['state']:12s} model={j.get('model','?'):15s} provider={j.get('provider','?'):12s}")
```

### Fixing api_key Issue

```python
import yaml
with open("/root/.hermes/config.yaml") as f:
    cfg = yaml.safe_load(f)
key = ""
with open("/root/.hermes/.env") as f:
    for line in f:
        if line.startswith("DEEPSEEK_API_KEY="):
            key = line.strip().split("=", 1)[1]
            break
if cfg.get("model", {}).get("api_key") is None and key:
    cfg["model"]["api_key"] = key
    with open("/root/.hermes/config.yaml", "w") as f:
        yaml.safe_dump(cfg, f)
```

### Deleting Jobs

```python
data["jobs"] = [
    j for j in data["jobs"]
    if not j["name"].startswith(("sol-", "ic-")) and j["name"] != "lead-scout-weekly"
]
with open("/root/.hermes/cron/jobs.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
```

## Publish Pipeline (npm Packages to GitHub Packages)

### Setup

Add to the base monorepo:

1. **`.changeset/config.json`:**
   ```json
   { "$schema": "https://unpkg.com/@changesets/config@3/schema.json",
     "changelog": "@changesets/cli/changelog", "commit": true,
     "access": "public", "baseBranch": "main", "updateInternalDependencies": "patch", "ignore": [] }
   ```

2. **`.github/workflows/publish.yml`** — triggered on push to main with changeset files or package.json changes. Uses `changesets/action@v1` with `publish: npx changeset publish`. Requires `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `packages: write` permission.

3. **`.npmrc`** in each consumer repo:
   ```
   @org:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com/
   //npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${GITHUB_TOKEN}
   ```

4. **First publish**: create a changeset with `npx changeset`, commit, push. The publish workflow creates a release PR. Merge it → packages published.

### Template Package Fix

Replace `file:` paths before pushing:
```python
for k in list(pkg.get("dependencies", {})):
    if k.startswith("@org/") and pkg["dependencies"][k].startswith("file:"):
        pkg["dependencies"][k] = "^0.1.0"
```

## Branch Protection & Security Hardening

### GitHub API Quirks for Branch Protection

**The `gh api` `-f` flag always sends strings**, even for booleans/integers. The branch protection API expects proper JSON types:
```bash
# WRONG - sends "true" as string, "1" as string → 422 error
gh api repos/Org/repo/branches/main/protection --method PUT \
  -f "required_pull_request_reviews[required_approving_review_count]=1"
```

```python
# RIGHT - sends actual JSON types
import json, subprocess
payload = {
    "required_status_checks": {"strict": True, "contexts": ["CI"]},
    "required_pull_request_reviews": {"required_approving_review_count": 1, "dismiss_stale_reviews": True},
    "enforce_admins": True,
    "restrictions": None
}
r = subprocess.run(["gh", "api", f"repos/Org/repo/branches/main/protection",
    "--method", "PUT", "--input", "-"],
    input=json.dumps(payload), capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=30)
```

### Removing Protection (to re-create)

```bash
gh api repos/Org/repo/branches/main/protection --method DELETE
```

### AI-Friendly Protection (no human gate, CI still runs)

```python
payload = {
    "required_status_checks": {"strict": False, "contexts": []},
    "required_pull_request_reviews": None,  # no human review
    "enforce_admins": None,
    "restrictions": None
}
```

This keeps status checks running but allows AI to push directly — no PR approval gates. Useful for fully automated workflows where AI commits directly to main.

### Environment Creation

```python
# Create environment (PUT -f works here for simple fields)
r = subprocess.run(["gh", "api", f"repos/Org/{repo}/environments/production",
    "--method", "PUT", "--input", "-"],
    input=json.dumps({"wait_timer": 0, "deployment_branch_policy": {"protected_branches": True}}),
    capture_output=True, timeout=30)
# Note: wait_timer requires a paid GitHub plan; remove it for free orgs
```

### Org-Level Secrets

```python
import subprocess
for name, value in {"VPS_HOST": "ip", "VPS_USER": "root", "VPS_KEY": "ssh-key"}:
    r = subprocess.run(["gh", "secret", "set", name, "--org", org, "--visibility", "all", "-b", value])
```

### Workflow Default Permissions

```python
# Set to read-only (more secure)
r = subprocess.run(["gh", "api", f"orgs/{org}/actions/permissions/workflow",
    "--method", "PUT", "--input", "-"],
    input=json.dumps({"default_workflow_permissions": "read", "can_approve_pull_request_reviews": False}))
```

### Local Repo Sync (finding and pushing unpushed changes)

After the centralized CI/CD rollout, client repos may have local-only changes:

```bash
# Find all repos with unpushed commits
for d in /root/repo1 /root/repo2; do
  if [ -d "$d/.git" ]; then
    cd "$d"
    ahead=$(git rev-list --count @{u}..HEAD 2>/dev/null)
    behind=$(git rev-list --count HEAD..@{u} 2>/dev/null)
    modified=$(git status --porcelain | wc -l)
    echo "$(basename $d): ahead=$ahead behind=$behind modified=$modified"
    cd /root
  fi
done

# Force push auto-generated sites (no other contributors)
git push --force origin main

# Pull-rebase for repos with branch protection
git pull origin main --rebase
git push origin main
```

Note: The `gh api repos/Org/repo/stats/contributors` endpoint returns **empty arrays** for repos where all commits came from automation (AI agents). This is expected — don't infer "dead repo" from empty contribution stats.

## Client Dependency Strategy: file: vs github: vs npm

### file: (Development Only)

```json
"@ai-whisperers/ui": "file:/root/ai-whisperers-base/packages/ui"
```

**Pros:** Instant sync, no publish needed, works offline.
**Cons:** Paths are machine-specific — can't commit for CI/CD. Triggers secret scanning if path contains `/root/`.
**Fix for CI/CD:** Use `file:../ai-whisperers-base/packages/ui` (relative to the monorepo clone location on the CI runner).

### github: (Private, No Registry)

```json
"@ai-whisperers/ui": "github:Ai-Whisperers/base"
```

**Pros:** Only org members can access. No npm registry needed. Works with `npm install`.
**Cons:** Requires a release tag (`#v0.1.0`). Nested paths aren't natively supported (can't do `github:org/repo#v1/packages/ui` cleanly).

### npm (GitHub Packages Registry)

```json
// .npmrc
@ai-whisperers:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com/
//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${GITHUB_TOKEN}

// package.json
"@ai-whisperers/ui": "^0.1.0"
```

**Pros:** Standard npm workflow. Version ranges. Works across any machine.
**Cons:** Requires GitHub token setup in every consumer. Changeset workflow needed for publishing.

### Recommended Multi-Tier Approach

| Context | Strategy |
|---------|----------|
| Local dev | `file:` (relative path) |
| CI/CD build | `github:` tag URL |
| External contributors | npm with GitHub Packages |
| Templates | `file:` during development, `github:` before commit |

### Client-to-Monorepo Conversion

For a client that clones from a template that used `file:` deps:

```bash
# Find file: deps
grep "file:" package.json

# Strip to version-only (for npm registry)
sed -i 's|"file:.*"|"^0.1.0"|g' package.json
# OR point to GitHub tag
sed -i 's|"file:.*"|"github:Ai-Whisperers/base#v0.1.0"|g' package.json
```

### Secret Scanning Push Protection

When pushing repos that had local `file:/root/paths` or `.env.example` files containing API keys:

**Error:** `Push cannot contain secrets` from `GH013` rule violation.

**Resolve:**
1. Find the secret: `git push origin main --force 2>&1 | grep "locations:" -A5`
2. Clean the offending commit from history:
   ```bash
   git checkout --orphan clean-branch
   git rm -rf .
   git checkout main -- desired-files
   git branch -D main
   git branch -m main
   git push origin main --force
   ```
3. If branch protection blocks force push, create a bypass with `-o` flag or disable protection temporarily from GitHub UI.
4. Add the offending file to `.gitignore` to prevent re-introduction.
